summary: Cathy O'Neil provides a positive health update following bariatric surgery, explaining her decision-making process and recovery progress. She describes her changing relationship with food and her goal of returning to cycling. The post reveals that the turning point came when she could no longer bike challenging hills, triggering fears of a destructive feedback loop of declining fitness. She addresses her identity as a 'fit fat person' and her long-term thinking about staying active into old age. The surgery was motivated both by pre-diabetic concerns and a desire to maintain an active lifestyle for decades to come.
key_points: A specific failure—being unable to face steep bike hills near Poughkeepsie—triggered the realization that declining fitness creates a self-reinforcing downward spiral
The decision to get bariatric surgery was driven by long-term thinking about health and mobility at age 74, not just immediate diabetes concerns
Everyone she interviewed about the surgery had the same regret: not doing it sooner
Her self-identity as a 'fit fat person' who could always point to her biking ability was disrupted when that became no longer true
Recovery is going well with careful eating, longer walks, and plans to return to cycling
money_quotes: When you don't bike regularly, you get out of shape, which makes it even harder to bike when the weather gets better.
Once I realized this destructive feedback loop was happening, it really bothered me, and moreover fucked with my long-held notion of myself as a fit fat person.
I started thinking long term about my health, and how I wanted to be one of those active old ladies, shouting and carrying on at protests.
Although I was carrying my 300 pound body pretty well at 44, it would be much harder to do that at 74.
Their biggest regret seemed to be that they hadn't done it earlier.
It's fun, as long as you don't make people listen to your work-out schedule or anything. If I start doing that, please slap me.
key_insight: Recognizing a destructive feedback loop in your health—where declining ability makes improvement harder—is the moment to intervene, not when you've hit bottom.
